paragraphs.body: A story about friendship, survival, loss, and finding your voice. Mckenna Mealy, the quiet girl who learned from an early age to keep her thoughts to herself and to say nothing, as her only means of survival. Is now faced with her first year of college and a deep secret that she is beginning to fear will cost her everything. As she tries to blend into her surroundings and start fresh, a boy from her past threatens all that she has been working to hide. As Wyatt Black intrudes her bubble, she realizes she is not the only one with scars from the past. As she witnesses Wyatt life starts to spiral out of control, she must make a choice between staying silent or speaking out for the people she loves, the life she wants, and the truth that needs to be heard.

annotations.text: Mckenna Mealy, the quiet girl who learned from an early age to keep her thoughts to herself and to say nothing, as her only means of survival. Is
annotations.comment: Not a full sentence.

paragraphs.body: Filling the Pages
For the readers learning how to carry your broken pieces with a hopeful heart remember there are people and places you still must meet.

annotations.comment: I like this dedication!

paragraphs.body: As I look outside my childhood bedroom, I am greeted with the familiar chipped green childhood playset and the fallen leaves scattered in the yard. I patiently wait for my alarm to go off knowing it will eventually greet me with the generic blaring sound I selected years ago.

annotations.comment: By the You did a nice job of evoking an adult leaving their childhood home.
annotations.context_left: As I look outside my childhood bedroom, I am greeted
annotations.context_right: familiar chipped green childhood playset and the fallen leaves scattered in the yard. I patiently wa

paragraphs.body: I settle into my covers soaking up my last remnants of my childhood room before I leave for college at Emory University. As I look around the four walls most familiar to me filled with memories of my childhood through teenage years, I feel a sense of longing that this is the last time I will be this version of myself.

annotations.comment: While I like this opening, I did want to move along from the happy memories a bit faster.

paragraphs.body: The chipped wall by the door where I rammed my doorknob repeatedly into when I was in a hurry, the small, mismatched paint color in the corner that my father painted when I drew all over the walls in sharpie. The four walls that held firm when the rest of my life felt so desolate and lonely and when my walls cracked, I could always count on these to stand firm.
The room I would always come back to where I felt the safest and understood in my own space away from the judgmental eyes of my father and peers where I could just be myself and that was enough.

annotations.comment: This is a nice insertion of the difficulties of this childhood, but I guess I wondered how safe this room could feel in the dad’s house?

paragraphs.body: My alarm blaring jolted me from my thoughts before my thoughts down memory lane started to become more depressing. Throwing my sage green comforter off and wrapping my arms around my frigid body I made my way downstairs to grab some coffee.

The cinnamon hues of cinnamon rolls led to the direction of the kitchen as I smiled to myself, my favorite. The rare sight of my father bent over to retrieve the cinnamon rolls caused a smile even larger to appear on my face until I saw the ankle monitor and the smile quickly dropped.
“Hey father,”

annotations.comment: I honestly never believe it when a character calls a parent “Father” or “Mother.” Sorry!

paragraphs.body: “Hi Sweetheart, ready for your big day?”
Slapping a big smile onto my face, I reply “Can’t wait” more like can’t wait to get away from this drama that has dominated the entirety of my highschool life.

annotations.comment: I think you might consider having her in this bad mood from when the alarm goes off. It seems off that she would kind of forget about this situation, you know?

paragraphs.body: Highschool was rough for me I was always sitting alone keeping my head down to get by. I didn’t have a lot of friends who wanted to hang out with the house arrest father. I could feel the stares and hear the murmurs as I would grace the halls of Nova Highschool also known as my personal hell.

annotations.comment: This backstory doesn’t work here. Keep us in the story.

paragraphs.body: Which sucked because I used to love school, I found so much relief in passing through the threshold of the building I had eight hours away from the suffocation I felt at home. As the newspaper published my father’s arrest in the paper school became my worst fear, everything I had cherished was uprooted and replaced with ugly self-loathing and hatred.

I felt as if I deserved the whispers of the bullying because I was a freak, the daughter of a felon, even though I had no impact on his choices that night. I’ve just had to deal with the consequences of his choice every day since.

annotations.comment: You might be able to work some of this into the scene — if, for example, he is not allowed to go with her to the airport or school?

paragraphs.body: Sitting at the kitchen table fiddling with my sleeve quietly while the hatred brews for the man reading the paper causally in the seat across from me sipping his coffee while his ankle monitor flashes red antagonizing me. Suddenly jolting I stand up and wash my dishes in the sink bouncing from heel to heel,
“I should probably get on the road I want to get there at a decent time.”
“Ok, let's grab the rest of your bags from upstairs so we can load the station wagon.”

Turning to walk up the stairs, I grab my last remaining bags and glance around the four walls that were my safe place and start to feel a little sad as I turn to retreat down the stairs, I meet my father's curious eyes.
Shrugging it off I bound down the stairs and out the front door to the beat-up station wagon that used to be my grandmother's and threw the last bag in.

Walking around to the driver’s seat I sat and start to connect the aux and navigation to Georgia the next seven hours with just my thoughts alone creates a pit in my stomach. My father starts tapping on the window.

annotations.comment: Yes, this is working better — keep developing this idea that she wants to be free of him and he wants to have a meaningful goodbye and neither of them are very good at emotions.

paragraphs.body:
Rolling the window down, “Hey Mckenna, I just want you to know I’m so proud of you and you are going to do great at college, I just want to talk to you about,
	Sharply cutting him off, “Yes, thank you father I’ll see you around.”
	A look of hurt crosses his face wrinkled with age and years of stress. Desperate to end this conversation and not ruin what started out as a good day, I rolled the window up, made a small wave and finally put the car into drive.

annotations.comment: Wow.

annotations.text: what started out as a good day,
annotations.comment: I can buy that she wants to get on her way and be free of him. I don’t quite buy that it was a good day.

paragraphs.body: My college in Georgia is eight hours away from my hometown in Virginia. A perfect distance far away from a town that made me feel trapped. I love the University I chose but the most appealing aspect was the distance. It’s a perfect day in August. The sun is so bright, and the blue sky is stripped with clouds. I think of all the possibilities at my new school, trying not to let self-doubt seep in.

I'm still writing this section- She drives to Emory University in Georgia to start her first year of college. She must move all her stuff into her dorm and meet her random roommate Layla who is super happy and bubbly. She is excited for the school year and Mckenna wants to feel the same so desperately. Layla is from the area and is invited to the bonfire by her boyfriend on the football team and invites Mckenna who hasn’t been invited to a party in years. Mckenna is super nervous about putting herself out there and making friends, but Layla’s upbeat energy finally convinces her.
“I knock my knees together and fold in my lips, feeling antsy. Out of place.

Wyatt Black is sitting across from me at a bonfire at Emory. Racking my brain trying to figure out why he is at this party. I remember people talking about him not liking the coach at his old college.

annotations.comment: Is this all happening in one day?

paragraphs.body: People always raved about him because he was one of the first football players to go D1 in fifteen years from our area. Blah Blah, He must have transferred to Emory... shit. I would recognize those eyes anywhere he’s been my secret crush since the fourth grade.

annotations.comment: I like the idea of her encountering someone from her hometown at this new place. What a drag to her new persona!
annotations.context_right: he was one of the f

paragraphs.body: I used to sit next to him at lunch in middle school. His mom would always write notes hoping that he was having a good day telling him how much she loved him and then I would watch him throw them away.

I was always so envious of him. My mom worked all the time, so I rarely saw her and when I did, she made it seem like it took time out of her day rather than prioritizing me for the little time we had.

annotations.comment: It’s a nice little detail to include (although, again, makes him seem like a jerk!).

paragraphs.body: Wyatt is seated on the bench across from me, his face going in and out of focus as I try to think about anything else other than the boy across from me. I’ve caught him staring at me a few times and I wonder what he feels when he looks at me. Disenchantment would be my guess, and pity taking second place.

annotations.comment: I’m not quite following why they wouldn’t greet each other? Does he not recognize her?

paragraphs.body: Mason is seated beside him, chugging down his fifth beer and acting loud and moronic. He’s living up to the football player stereotype just as effortlessly as I embrace my tragic shy outcast persona, so I can’t really judge his character.

I watch as Wyatt reaches into the cooler beside him before I pull my eyes away and redirect my attention to my hands fisted tightly in my lap. I circle my thumbs zeroing in on my still-chipped nail polish.

I’m so immersed in my fingernails that I fail to notice Wyatt has left Mason’s side and is now taking a seat to my left. It’s a small bench so our shoulders brush. Warmth seeps through the thick sleeve of my hoodie as his scent surrounds me, peppermint gum mixed in with firewood smoke.

He hands me a beer. I curl my fingers around the ice-cold beverage. “Thanks,” I say, looking at him. He’s clasping a red solo cup most likely filled with the beer from the keg. “So how have you been Mckenna this isn’t really your scene? I never saw you at parties in highschool.” He takes a sip from his cup, eyes pinned on me over the rim.

annotations.comment: Would they begin this intimately?

paragraphs.body: I watch this throat bob as he swallows a gulp, then swing my gaze back up. “I guess I am trying something new.” Wringing my hands on my lap I felt the redness appear on my cheeks.
“Mhm trying something new,” he chuckled and threw down another gulp of his beer. “What else is on this list?”

A giggle comes out of me, unexpectedly. Untethered and unplanned. Trying to respond and seem like question didn’t surprise me. “Make some friends, see what the world has to offer except for our hometown.”
Nodding his head he is aware of my father's jail sentence, I am sure; it headlined our local newspaper.

Giving him an awkward shrug, he responds, “I can’t imagine how hard it was for you to experience all that shit you went through back then. I know it probably got worse after the trial and I was a couple of years older and wasn’t in town that March.”

annotations.comment: ?Okay, so my one question at this point is what the conflict in this story is going to be? She has a problem — a bad HS experience. But now, everything seems to have worked out pretty well?

annotations.text: and I was a couple of years older and wasn’t in town that March.”
annotations.comment: Little awkward.

paragraphs.body: My mouth snaps shut when his answer sinks in. No one ever asks for cares about how hard it was for me. They only ever ridicule me for being a felon's daughter or an accomplice to his crime.

annotations.comment: It might be helpful to show that scene earlier, somehow.

paragraphs.body: Wyatt looks so relaxed and, in his element, as he stares out into the party and the blaze of the fire. He’s managed to render me speechless, which is rare, and I don’t think I like it. Swallowing, I fumble for something/anything to say but come up empty.

I pop open the tab of my beer and start to chug. It fizzes on my tongue, as I remember my voice, “So, are you actually friends with these guys? I flick my hand up and wave it around, referencing Mason and all the other meatheads around the fire.
Before Max can respond, Mason interrupts roaring with laughter slapping his buddy on the back challenging him to shot gun at the other side of the fire.

annotations.comment: Who’s Max?

paragraphs.body: I groan inwardly, hoping Wyatt isn’t friends with those guys.

“Nope,” Max mutters, shifting on the bench, “I just transferred here so I am just feeling out the new guys, but those ones will definitely not fall into the category of what I consider to be friends.”
“Gotcha, are you liking Emory? Where did you go before I can’t remember?”

“Yes, it's a good school, he replies, I went to a school in North Carolina the coach's values were horrible it wasn’t a good experience I decided to take a semester off in the spring and transferred here in the fall.”

Nodding back at him until a loud echo of laughter distracts us from our conversation, I look among the crowd trying to spot Layla. I see her blonde curly hair sneaking away with Adam her boyfriend, I don’t want to leave her, but I also want out of this conversation.

annotations.text: also want out of this conversation.
annotations.comment: I’m not quite sure why this conversation is one she wants out of?

paragraphs.body: Wyatt is watching me eager for my reply, this is probably the part where I’m supposed to smile back at him, or say something flirty, or ask what his plans are for the night.
But in true Mckenna fashion, I ditch him like a coward.

I stand up from the bench, I stutter through a goodbye and offer a quick wave. “Sorry I gotta go I am super tired from the long drive today. Bye.” I catch the way he is surprised by my quick goodbye and his disappointment while I am fleeing from the bonfire.

It is a short walk back to campus. I remember the path following Layla a couple hours earlier. I sent her a quick text letting her know that I left the bonfire and to have fun with Adam that I am super tired from the drive and need to get ready for bed. I make it to the middle of campus when I see the beautiful pond in the center and stop.

“Ugh why do I always do that; I need to stop running from social interaction.” I whisper to the night so disappointed in myself for running away at the first person to show me kindness in so long. I watch the fountain water ripple and lap beneath the starlight, a picture of tranquility. It looks so peaceful, so unburdened. Envy spools and coils in my chest because...
I want that. I want that more than anything.
Peace.
Just one peaceful moment. Lots of people get thousands of peaceful moments and all I want is one.
Just one.
As I exhale deeply, my hands instinctively wrap around my waist holding myself before looking back over the water as the moon offers a glimmer of contentment to the sky.

annotations.comment: This is a really nice little moment. So, Evelyn, you’re off to a good start! I enjoyed this excerpt and ended up liking that you had all of this happen in one (very long) day. I think that sort of marathon makes sense for having her end up just exhausted and wanting out by the end. In general, you’re having your narrator assert things to readers instead of showing us in teh scene. Your scenes tend to be fairly bland — people don’t have much to say to each other — and then your narrator fills in the emotional terrain for us. But I think your scenes can do both. What happens, for example, if you take what you already have there — the dad trying too hard, but not being able to go with his daughter — and have that influence what they do and say a bit more? Maybe they even argue a little bit about the situation they’re in. And then at the bonfire, maybe she actually is tired enough to be a little bitchy — so we see how things go wrong between her and Max/Wyatt instead of just being told they have. More broadly, I’m not yet seeing the overall plot of this book. As I said, things seem to be sort of working themselves out for our heroine. What’s the conflict going to be? Great work! Happy writing!
